graph.confusable_reason: Both are members of the "Septoria leaf blotch complex" of wheat (historically Septoria nodorum and Septoria tritici), produce tan-to-brown necrotic leaf blotches with chlorotic halos and embedded dark fruiting bodies (pycnidia), spread by rain-splash, and frequently co-occur in the same field and even the same lesion. Field distinction by gross lesion appearance alone is unreliable; the resolving feature is the pycnidial morphology and whether glume blotch develops on the head.
graph.shared_presentation: Oval-to-lens-shaped tan/brown necrotic blotches on lower and mid-canopy leaves, often with a yellow (chlorotic) margin, coalescing under prolonged leaf wetness and progressing upward through the canopy; dark pycnidia embedded in dead tissue; both favored by cool-to-mild, wet/humid weather and dense stands, and both are residue-borne and rain-splash dispersed.

forks.question: Inspect the pycnidia in the lesion under a hand lens (10-20x) or after incubation in a moist chamber: what color/size are they and what exudes from them?
forks.a_signal: Pycnidia are smaller, light to honey-brown, and ooze pinkish/cream cirrhi; conidia are short, hyaline with few (typically 1-3) septa
forks.b_signal: Pycnidia are larger, dark brown to black, conspicuous in straw-colored lesions, and ooze whitish cirrhi; conidia are long, thread-like (filiform) with 3-7 septa

forks.question: Examine the wheat head/glumes at and after flowering for blotch symptoms.
forks.a_signal: Causes glume blotch: chocolate-brown to purplish-brown discoloration of glumes and the head, often shriveling grain (a defining feature)
forks.b_signal: Essentially restricted to leaves and leaf sheaths; does not cause true glume blotch on the head

forks.question: What is the dominant color and shape of the early leaf lesion?
forks.a_signal: Lens-shaped tan-brown blotches, often with a more diffuse chlorotic-brown center and yellow halo
forks.b_signal: Irregular gray-green to tan blotches that bleach to a light straw/ash color, frequently bounded by leaf veins giving a rectangular/blocky outline

forks.question: What temperature regime and latitude/season favored the outbreak?
forks.a_signal: Favored by warmer, humid conditions; relatively more important in warmer/southern and warm-humid wheat regions
forks.b_signal: Favored by cooler, prolonged-wet conditions; dominant in cool, maritime and northern/high-rainfall wheat regions and early-season cool springs

forks.question: How long after infection do symptoms and visible pycnidia appear (latent period)?
forks.a_signal: Shorter latent period; symptoms and pycnidia develop relatively quickly after wet periods
forks.b_signal: Longer latent period (often 2-3+ weeks) so lesions appear well after the favorable wet weather, complicating spray timing

graph.narrative_a: Parastagonospora nodorum (Stagonospora nodorum blotch, SNB) produces lens-shaped tan-brown leaf blotches with yellow halos that progress up the canopy, but its hallmark is glume blotch: chocolate-brown discoloration of the glumes and head causing shriveled grain. Pycnidia are smaller and light honey-brown, exuding pinkish cirrhi, and conidia are short and few-septate. It tends to be favored by warmer, humid conditions and is seed- and residue-borne. Effectors interacting with host Snn/Tsn susceptibility genes drive necrosis.
graph.narrative_b: Zymoseptoria tritici (Septoria tritici blotch, STB) is a leaf-only disease causing gray-green blotches that bleach to ash/straw color, often blocky and vein-delimited, packed with conspicuous dark-brown to black pycnidia. Conidia are long, filiform, and multi-septate. It is favored by cool, prolonged-wet/maritime conditions, has a long latent period that decouples symptom appearance from the infection event, and does not produce glume blotch. It is a major fungicide-resistance concern (azole/SDHI/QoI shifts).
graph.management_a: Use clean/treated seed and resistant cultivars; rotate away from wheat and bury/manage residue to reduce inoculum. Apply foliar fungicides (triazoles; SDHI/QoI mixes) timed to protect the flag leaf and head, since glume blotch directly hits yield/quality. Avoid excessive nitrogen and dense canopies; in fields with known Tsn1 susceptibility, prioritize SNB-tolerant varieties.
graph.management_b: Plant STB-resistant cultivars and use residue management/rotation to lower splash inoculum; delay early dense canopies. Time flag-leaf (GS37-39) fungicides to protect the upper leaves, accounting for the long latent period. Critically, rotate fungicide modes of action and use mixtures/multisites (e.g., add chlorothalonil where permitted) because Z. tritici has widespread resistance to QoIs, eroding azole sensitivity, and emerging SDHI resistance.

lay.farmer_lay_report: The lower and middle leaves have oval-to-football-shaped tan and brown dead blotches, often with a yellow halo around the edges. In the wet, humid weather the spots are running together and the damage is creeping up toward the upper leaves, worst in the thick, lush parts of the stand.
lay.decisive_lay_a: Look in the dead tan tissue for tiny pinhead specks that are only light tan to honey-brown; in damp weather they ooze out a pinkish or cream-colored ooze.
lay.decisive_lay_b: Look in the straw-colored dead tissue for larger, very dark brown-to-black pinhead specks that stand out clearly; in damp weather they ooze out a whitish ooze.
